moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Hungarian Opening, an offbeat yet sophisticated choice that immediately signals we're not playing for a typical center clash. By fianchettoing our kingside bishop on g2, we control the long diagonal and establish a flexible position where we're happy to let Black dictate the center first. This move embraces a 'slow chess' philosophy—we're patient, we're strong along light squares, and we're ready to adapt. The beauty of g3 is that it keeps all our options open while making it psychologically harder for Black to use standard Sicilian tactics.

Black immediately stakes a claim in the center with this Sicilian thrust, inviting us to occupy d4 or challenge the pawn. Rather than meeting this with the expected 2. d4 (which would transpose into standard Sicilian positions), we've set up the Hungarian to encourage Black into familiar ideas while we develop our unique approach. By playing c5, Black is essentially saying they want an open, tactical game—but you should be ready to show that the fianchetto setup can neutralize these ambitions and turn the board into a strategic battleground where patience and piece placement outweigh wild tactics.
